expectations.text: answers-spike-vs-build-question: The output explicitly answers the user's question: 'should we spike, or just build?' — either with a justified 'no spike needed' note in decision.md, or by producing a full spike.
expectations.evidence: decision.md TL;DR: 'Spike, don't just build. The original framing — "should we just start building?" — would commit weeks of engineering to a plan whose foundation has not been tested end-to-end.' assumptions.md also has a 'Should we spike?' section answering 'Yes.' A full spike is produced under spike/spike.py.

expectations.text: assumptions-list-exists: An assumptions list file is present in outputs.
expectations.evidence: outputs/assumptions.md exists (6286 bytes), titled '# Assumptions — LLM-as-recommender spike'.

expectations.text: assumptions-categorized: Assumptions are categorized as must-hold / should-hold / nice-if-it-holds.
expectations.evidence: assumptions.md has explicit sections: '## Must hold (project does not exist if false — spike these)', '## Should hold (architecture changes if false — log as risks; test only if cheap)', and '## Nice if it holds (don't spike)'.

expectations.text: must-hold-count-2-to-4: There are between 2 and 4 must-hold assumptions.
expectations.evidence: assumptions.md lists exactly 4 must-hold assumptions: M1 Output shape reliability, M2 Ranking quality vs. baseline, M3 Latency and cost envelope, M4 End-to-end information architecture walkthrough.

expectations.text: each-must-hold-has-measurable-gate: Each must-hold assumption has a measurable, falsifiable gate.
expectations.evidence: M1 gate: '≥ 9/10 hand-picked user histories produce a parseable response where the list has length 20, all entries are unique, and all entries are members of the supplied catalog.' M2 gate: 'LLM\'s top-5 overlaps the existing recommender\'s top-20 by mean ≥ 2/5 (i.e. ≥ 40% recall@5)' AND 'human reviewer judges... ≥ 7/10 cases.' M3 gate: 'Median end-to-end latency ≤ 5 seconds OR ≤ 30 seconds. Median per-call cost is ≤ $0.02.' M4 gate: 'Spike runs end-to-end on at least one short-history user (≤ 5 events) and one long-history user (≥ 50 events) without ad-hoc fixes.' All gates are numeric/falsifiable.

expectations.text: single-file-spike-exists: A single-file spike exists whose filename contains 'spike'. No package structure beneath outputs/.
expectations.evidence: outputs/spike/spike.py is the single Python file (17104 bytes). The spike/ subdirectory contains only spike.py and a README.md (documentation, not code). No __init__.py, no separate baseline.py / metrics.py modules — the entire spike (config, catalog, fixtures, prompt, gates, parsing, IA walkthrough, main) lives in one file.

expectations.text: spike-uses-real-sdks: The spike imports the real Anthropic SDK and uses real call shapes.
expectations.evidence: spike.py line 40: 'from anthropic import Anthropic'. Lines 326, 336-342: 'client = Anthropic(api_key=API_KEY)' and 'client.messages.create(model=MODEL, max_tokens=MAX_TOKENS, temperature=TEMPERATURE, system=SYSTEM_PROMPT, messages=[{"role": "user", "content": user_msg}])'. Real SDK, real call shape including resp.usage.input_tokens / output_tokens for cost calc.

expectations.text: spike-no-production-scaffolding: The spike file has no Pydantic models, no API server scaffolding, no separate config files.
expectations.evidence: Only imports are stdlib (json, os, re, sys, time, dataclasses, pathlib) plus 'from anthropic import Anthropic'. No pydantic, fastapi, flask, or similar imports. Config is via env vars + module-level constants. The single dataclass GateResult is a plain Python dataclass for results, not a Pydantic model. spike/ subdirectory has only spike.py and README.md (no separate config.py / settings.toml / pyproject.toml).

expectations.text: tradeoffs-md-exists: A `tradeoffs.md` file exists in outputs.
expectations.evidence: outputs/tradeoffs.md exists (11376 bytes), titled '# tradeoffs.md — LLM-as-recommender spike'.

expectations.text: tradeoffs-has-five-row-structure: `tradeoffs.md` rows have the assumption / gate / result / learning / pivot structure for each must-hold assumption.
expectations.evidence: Each of M1, M2, M3, M4 in tradeoffs.md has all five sections in order: '- **Assumption.** ...', '- **Gate.** ...', '- **Result.** _To be filled..._' (or already-surfaced learning for M4), '- **Learning.** _To be filled..._' (or actual learnings for M4), '- **Pivot.**' (followed by a table of result→pivot mappings). Format note at top: 'Format follows the eng-feasibility skill: assumption / gate / result / learning / pivot, one block per must-hold.'

expectations.text: explicit-decision-stated: An explicit decision is stated: build / pivot architecture / pivot narrative / stop.
expectations.evidence: decision.md '## Decision class (per the skill)' says explicitly: '**Pivot architecture — conditional on running the spike.**' Three structural changes from the original plan are enumerated (candidate-gen step, separate cold-start path, deterministic post-filter for purchase exclusion). decision.md also includes a 'Why not "just build"' and 'Why not "stop"' section to bound the choice.

expectations.text: ranking-quality-is-must-hold: A must-hold assumption captures the ranking-quality bet (e.g., 'LLM produces a stable, parseable ranked list of 20 item IDs that is at least as good as the existing pipeline on a held-out evaluation'). The gate is measurable (recall@20, NDCG, or similar).
expectations.evidence: M2 in assumptions.md is exactly the ranking-quality bet: 'The LLM\'s top-20 has at least comparable top-of-list relevance to the existing recommender on the same user histories.' Gate: 'mean ≥ 2/5 (i.e. ≥ 40% recall@5 vs. the production system) AND a human reviewer judges the LLM\'s top-5 "plausible or better than baseline" on ≥ 7/10 cases. Both halves must hold.' This is a numeric recall@5 threshold, not vague language. Implemented in spike.py evaluate() function which computes overlap of top5 with baseline_set: 'recall_at_5 = overlap / 5 if top5 else 0.0'.

expectations.text: latency-or-cost-considered: Latency or per-call cost is captured as either a must-hold or should-hold assumption with a numeric threshold.
expectations.evidence: M3 in assumptions.md ('Latency and cost envelope') is a must-hold with explicit numeric thresholds: 'Median end-to-end latency across the 10 histories is ≤ 5 seconds (online-with-cache feasible) OR ≤ 30 seconds (precompute-only feasible). Median per-call cost is ≤ $0.02 at the chosen model. Outside both buckets ⇒ architecture pivot or stop.' The spike measures latency via time.monotonic() and computes est_cost_usd from token usage with PRICE_INPUT_PER_MTOK = 3.0 / PRICE_OUTPUT_PER_MTOK = 15.0.

claims.claim: The spike has not been executed because no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is available in this environment.
claims.evidence: tradeoffs.md status block: 'Spike code is written and runnable (spike/spike.py). It has not been executed in this environment because no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is available.' Consistent with the eval prompt instruction 'Don't actually call any real APIs'. spike.py lines 47-49 implement a hard-fail: 'if not API_KEY: sys.exit("missing ANTHROPIC_API_KEY (no credentials => no measurement)")' — confirms it cannot have produced fake numbers.

claims.claim: All four must-hold gates are measurable / falsifiable, not vibes.
claims.evidence: M1 (≥9/10 with parse/length/unique/in-catalog), M2 (recall@5 ≥ 0.40 AND human ≥7/10), M3 (≤5s or ≤30s median, ≤$0.02), M4 (spike runs cleanly on a ≤5-event and a ≥50-event fixture). Each has either a count, ratio, or runtime threshold.

claims.claim: The IA walkthrough produced architectural pivots before any API call.
claims.evidence: tradeoffs.md M4 'Learning' section enumerates three pivots: cold-start as a separate non-LLM path, candidate-gen step in front of the LLM, purchase exclusion as deterministic post-filter. decision.md repeats these three under 'Pivot architecture'. spike.py walkthrough_check() implements the cold-start branching (events < 5).

claims.claim: The spike file uses only stdlib + the real Anthropic SDK (no production framework scaffolding).
claims.evidence: Imports are: json, os, re, sys, time, dataclasses, pathlib, anthropic. No fastapi, flask, pydantic, sqlalchemy, etc. The single dataclass is for in-memory results, not API contract.

claims.claim: The cost-per-call estimate is computed from real usage tokens, not hardcoded.
claims.evidence: spike.py evaluate(): 'cost = (in_tok / 1_000_000) * PRICE_INPUT_PER_MTOK + (out_tok / 1_000_000) * PRICE_OUTPUT_PER_MTOK'. Tokens come from resp.usage.input_tokens / resp.usage.output_tokens. Pricing constants are clearly marked as 'order-of-magnitude' and overrideable when model changes.

uncertainties: Spike could not be executed because no ANTHROPIC_API_KEY was available — so M1/M2/M3 have no measured results yet, only the gates and conditional pivots.
Baseline_top20 is a category-biased stand-in (_baseline()), not a snapshot of the real production recommender. M2 numbers from this run alone would not be conclusive for the recall leg.
Catalog is pinned at 200 items; if production candidate pool is larger, S2 escalates to a must-hold.
needs_review: Engineer with credentials still needs to run spike.py, paste roll-up numbers into the four 'Result' sections of tradeoffs.md, and do the human-judgment leg of M2.
workarounds: tradeoffs.md cannot fill in Result/Learning rows without an API call, so it ships with placeholders and a status block flagging the gap; conditional pivot tables let the next engineer read off the consequence directly once they have measurements.

suggestions.reason: Currently passes by absence (no pydantic / api server). A spike that imports anthropic but also imports e.g. typer for a CLI, or wraps the call in an async server, would still technically lack pydantic/server-scaffolding under a strict reading. Consider tightening to 'no third-party imports beyond anthropic + stdlib' or 'no class hierarchies / abstractions worth keeping beyond a single results dataclass' to make the assertion harder to satisfy by accident.

A spike subdirectory with spike.py + README.md passes, which is the right call. But the assertion would also pass if the spike imported helper modules placed elsewhere in outputs/ (e.g., outputs/util.py). Consider also asserting 'the spike file's imports do not reference any local modules in outputs/' to catch sneaky multi-file decomposition.

No assertion checks that the prompt actually constrains the output to a fixed JSON shape (e.g., a 'system prompt forbids preamble / specifies length' check). M1 in the eval focuses on the runtime gate, but a spike that defines M1 yet ships a system prompt that asks for prose would still pass all assertions and only fail at run time. Worth adding.

No assertion checks that the spike file's measurement code actually computes the metric named in the must-hold gate (e.g., recall@5 in M2 should appear as a function in spike.py). Would catch a spike that talks about recall@5 in tradeoffs.md but never measures it.

eval_feedback.overall: Assertions are well-targeted and the must-hold-gate / ranking-quality / latency-or-cost assertions are appropriately strict. Main suggestion: tighten the no-production-scaffolding and single-file assertions to guard against decomposition into helper modules and against soft 'a spike that doesn't actually measure the gate' failure modes.
